name: Polymorphic Inline Caches
summary: Hölzle, Chambers, Ungar (ECOOP 1991): cache multiple type-dispatch results per call site, with a small linear search through cached cases. Originally for Self; descended into V8 ICs, SpiderMonkey caches, Julia method tables. O(1) for <10 types; megamorphic cliff for more.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Urs Hölzle, Craig Chambers, and David Ungar (ECOOP 1991) introduced **Polymorphic Inline Caches**: cache multiple type-dispatch results per call site, with a small linear search through cached cases. Originally developed for the Self language, the technique propagated to every modern dynamic-language JIT.

The mechanism extends monomorphic inline caching: instead of one cached `(type, target)` pair, the cache holds a small list. On each call, walk the list; if the current type matches a cached entry, dispatch to the cached target. The list is bounded (typically 4–10 entries); when the list fills, the cache transitions to megamorphic mode and falls back to dynamic-lookup slow path.

sources.label: Polymorphic Inline Caches (Hölzle, Chambers, Ungar, ECOOP 1991)

sections.title: Production usage
sections.content:
PICs descended into V8 ICs, SpiderMonkey caches, JavaScriptCore inline caches, and Julia's method table dispatch. The technique is the standard solution to "dynamic dispatch in a statically-traced JIT": observe a few common types, generate fast paths for them, fall back to dispatch when types diverge.

The size of the cache list matters. Too small (3 or fewer): polymorphic code falls megamorphic too quickly. Too large (20+): linear search becomes expensive enough that the cached fast path doesn't pay off. Production engines tune this size empirically per call-site type, with smaller caches for types that are usually monomorphic and larger ones for types known to be naturally polymorphic.
